pattern: Flatten the m×n grid into a 1-D array, rotate it right by (k mod m*n) positions using slice arithmetic, then reshape back into m rows of n elements.
hints: A single shift moves the last element to the front and slides everything else right by one. With many shifts, think about what position each element ends up at after k steps — no need to simulate one step at a time.
After k shifts, the element originally at flat index i lands at flat index (i + k) % (m*n). Equivalently, flatten, then slice: new_flat = flat[-(k % total):] + flat[:-(k % total)].
Once you have the rotated flat array, chunk it into groups of n to rebuild the 2-D grid.

edge_inputs.description: k equals total cells, full wrap — grid unchanged
k larger than total cells, effective shift is k mod size
1x1 grid, any k leaves it unchanged
single row, k shifts wrap within the row then back
single column, shift by 1 moves last element to top
k is a multiple of total cells but not zero
negative-valued cells — sign must be preserved

stress.description: 50x50 grid with k up to 100 — checks reshape correctness at max dimensions
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    m = size
    n = size
    grid = [[rng.randint(-1000, 1000) for _ in range(n)] for _ in range(m)]
    k = rng.randint(0, 100)
    return [grid, k]

solutions.python:
class Solution:
    def shiftGrid(self, grid, k):
        m, n = len(grid), len(grid[0])
        flat = [v for row in grid for v in row]
        total = m * n
        shift = k % total
        flat = flat[-shift:] + flat[:-shift] if shift else flat
        return [flat[i * n:(i + 1) * n] for i in range(m)]
